original_hypothesis.title: Excess ETH from Buy Overpayment Permanently Locked in Curves Contract
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: _buyCurvesToken checks `if (msg.value < price + totalFee) revert InsufficientPayment()` but never refunds the difference when msg.value > price + totalFee. Any excess ETH remains permanently locked in the Curves contract with no extraction mechanism.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Users or integrating contracts may send more ETH than required. Without refund logic, overpayments are permanent losses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: msg.value >= price + totalFee check with no refund of excess; all fee transfers use exact amounts, not remaining balance

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Excess msg.value stays in contract permanently
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No refund transfer of (msg.value - price - totalFee) to msg.sender

analysis_questions: Do any integration contracts or frontends send excess ETH?
Is there any admin function to rescue stuck ETH?
validation_requirements: Send excess ETH on buy, verify contract balance increases by excess amount
Verify no mechanism to extract locked ETH

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: User estimates buy price but price increases slightly due to concurrent buy. User sends 10% extra for safety. The 10% excess is permanently locked.
prerequisites: User sends more ETH than exact price+fees

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of overpaid ETH per affected transaction
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user or contract that overpays on buy

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for excess ETH handling on buy

validation_result.mechanism: missing excess ETH refund on buy
validation_result.impact_summary: Any ETH sent above the exact price + totalFee during a buy operation is permanently retained by the Curves contract with no refund mechanism, resulting in direct user fund loss.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _buyCurvesToken function at Curves.sol line 218 checks `if (msg.value < price + totalFee) revert InsufficientPayment()` which permits any msg.value >= price + totalFee. The subsequent _transferFees function distributes exactly the computed fee amounts (protocolFee, subjectFee, referralFee, holderFee) to their respective destinations and retains the `price` portion in the contract to back the bonding curve. The difference `msg.value - price - totalFee` is never refunded to the buyer. There is no withdrawal function, no sweep function, and no receive/fallback forwarding in Curves.sol that could extract this excess ETH. While the excess ETH technically sits in the contract balance alongside bonding curve backing ETH, it is untracked and unattributable — no user can reclaim it.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 218-224 of Curves.sol clearly show the >= check without refund. The _transferFees function (lines 197-243) sends exact fee amounts using computed values, not msg.value remainder. No refund transfer exists anywhere in the buy path.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Line 221 uses `<` comparison allowing msg.value > price + totalFee. No subsequent code refunds the excess (msg.value - price - totalFee) to msg.sender. The _transferFees function sends only exact computed fee amounts, not the remaining balance.

For buy operations (isBuy=true), the function sends exact computed values: buyValue to protocolFeeDestination, subjectFee to curvesTokenSubject, referralFee to referral (if defined), and holderFee to feeRedistributor. Total outflow = totalFee. The price portion stays in contract. Any excess (msg.value - price - totalFee) is never sent anywhere.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls buyCurvesToken(subject, amount) with msg.value
3. _buyCurvesToken computes price = getPrice(supply, amount) at line 218
4. _buyCurvesToken computes totalFee via getFees(price) at line 219
5. Check at line 221: if (msg.value < price + totalFee) revert — passes if msg.value >= price + totalFee
6. Balance and supply updated at lines 223-224
7. _transferFees called at line 225 — sends exactly totalFee worth of ETH to fee destinations
8. Function returns — excess (msg.value - price - totalFee) remains in contract balance, never refunded
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is straightforward: _buyCurvesToken (line 221) allows overpayment via `<` check. _transferFees (lines 204-213 for buy path) distributes only computed fee amounts. The `price` stays in the contract for bonding curve backing. No code path exists to refund msg.value - price - totalFee. The contract has no withdrawal function for stuck ETH. The excess is permanently locked.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that contradict this finding. The defect is in the core buy logic regardless of configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A user calls buyCurvesToken with msg.value = 1 ETH when the actual cost (price + totalFee) is 0.5 ETH. The function passes the check at line 221 (1 ETH >= 0.5 ETH). _transferFees distributes the fee portions (e.g., 0.05 ETH total fees). The bonding curve price (0.45 ETH) stays in the contract. The excess 0.5 ETH also stays in the contract. No refund is issued. The user has permanently lost 0.5 ETH. This is especially dangerous for smart contract integrations that estimate prices with a buffer, or for users submitting transactions during volatile periods where price may change between estimation and execution.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Every overpayment on any buy transaction results in permanent ETH loss for the buyer. While individual losses depend on the overpayment amount, the cumulative effect across all users is significant. Smart contract integrations are particularly vulnerable as they may intentionally overpay to handle price fluctuations. There is no recovery mechanism — no admin withdrawal, no user refund function, and the excess ETH becomes commingled with bonding curve backing funds.
recommended_mitigations: Add refund logic after _transferFees: `uint256 excess = msg.value - price - totalFee; if (excess > 0) { (bool refundSuccess, ) = msg.sender.call{value: excess}(""); require(refundSuccess); }`
Alternatively, change the check to exact payment: `if (msg.value != price + totalFee) revert InsufficientPayment();`
Consider adding a sweep function for any accidentally locked ETH (with proper access control)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Curves with factory and FeeSplitter. 2. Subject creates curve by calling buyCurvesToken(subject, 1) with exact price. 3. Second user calls buyCurvesToken(subject, 1) with msg.value = 2x the required amount. 4. Check contract balance: it should contain the bonding curve backing PLUS the excess overpayment. 5. Verify no function exists to extract the excess. 6. Confirm the user received only their tokens, not a refund.
uncertainties: The excess ETH comingles with bonding curve backing ETH, so in theory it could be partially consumed by future sell payouts — but this would create a solvency issue for the bonding curve over time rather than a refund mechanism
additional_testing_needed: Quantify cumulative ETH lockup under realistic usage patterns with varying overpayment amounts
Test interaction with smart contract buyers that use gas estimation buffers
